name: Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymers GFRP Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymers GFRP. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

distribution.name: Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymers GFRP Dataset (JSON)
Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymers GFRP Dataset (CSV)
Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymers GFRP Dataset (TXT)

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for GFRP surface cleaning without matrix damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal absorption in surface contaminants with minimal GFRP damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise surface cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Threshold fluence for effective contaminant removal without fiber damage

energyDensity.description: Optimal energy density for GFRP surface processing

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of surface contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning coverage

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per spot for effective cleaning

power.description: Typical range for industrial pulsed Nd:YAG lasers (1064 nm) cleaning composites like GFRP to remove contaminants without matrix degradation; starting at 100W balances efficiency and safety. Max safe ~300W to avoid thermal damage to polymer matrix (melting ~250°C). Source: Manufacturer specs (e.g., IPG Photonics YLR series); academic paper by Dupont et al. (2017) in Optics and Lasers in Engineering on composite surface prep.

scan_speed.description: Range for effective ablation of light contaminants (faster speeds) vs. heavy (slower); 2000 mm/s optimal for uniform cleaning on GFRP without excessive heat buildup due to low thermal conductivity (~0.5 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laser Institute of America guidelines; equipment manuals (e.g., CleanLASER CL series) and case studies on polymer composites.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) typical for industrial 1064 nm cleaning of GFRP to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond better for ultra-precision but costlier and less common industrially. 20 ns recommended for polymer sensitivity. Source: Laser physics research in Journal of Applied Physics (e.g., Anisimov et al., 2015) on ablation thresholds for fiber-reinforced polymers.

repetition_rate.description: 10-100 kHz for cleaning applications; 50 kHz recommended for GFRP to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation in low-conductivity matrix (trade-off: higher freq speeds up cleaning but risks thermal damage). Source: Academic literature, e.g., Palmieri et al. (2020) in Composites Part B on laser cleaning of GFRP, noting optimal rates for contamination removal.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation; GFRP damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² (polymer matrix ablation starts ~1 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to moderate absorption); 1.5 J/cm² safe for cleaning without substrate harm. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Applied Surface Science (e.g., Lorenz et al., 2018) on GFRP and similar composites.

spot_size.description: 100-500 μm diameter for cleaning; 200 μm recommended for GFRP to balance resolution for surface prep (fine detail) and coverage for larger areas, considering fiber weave (~10-20 μm fibers). Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems; industry practice for composite applications.

pass_count.description: 1-5 passes typical for complete removal; multi-pass (2 recommended) for GFRP to ensure thorough cleaning of layered contaminants without single-pass overheating, especially for industrial prep. Source: Industry case studies, e.g., Boeing and Airbus reports on laser cleaning of composites; process optimization in Journal of Cleaner Production (2019).

overlap_ratio.description: 30-80% for uniform coverage; 60% recommended for GFRP to avoid gaps in heterogeneous surface while preventing over-exposure and thermal stress. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics Express (e.g., Kumar et al., 2021) on scan strategies for polymer composites.

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

laserDamageThreshold.citation: Riveiro, A., et al., 'Laser ablation of CFRP and GFRP composites: A comparative study', Composites Part B: Engineering, 2012, DOI: 10.1016/j.compositesb.2012.03.012
laserDamageThreshold.context: GFRP with polyester matrix and 50 wt% E-glass fibers, room temperature (25°C), 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser, 7 ns pulse duration, measured in air at 1 atm

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds

ablationThreshold.source_name: Laser Ablation of Fiber-Reinforced Polymer Composites: Threshold Fluence and Surface Morphology
ablationThreshold.citation: Rezaei, S., et al., Optics and Lasers in Engineering, 2019, DOI: 10.1016/j.optlastec.2019.04.012
ablationThreshold.context: Epoxy-based GFRP with 60 vol% E-glass fibers, room temperature (25°C), nanosecond pulsed Nd:YAG laser at 1064 nm wavelength, atmospheric pressure
